evidence: Package list (api/, config/, model/, model/exploration/, exploration/, query/, indexer/, utils/, viz/) maps directly to domain capabilities — a new engineer can predict each package's content from its name alone.

API layer is decomposed into five focused handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) composed by a thin WordSketchApiServer router.
GrammarConfig (immutable data) is separated from GrammarConfigLoader (file I/O) — textbook data/loader split with explicit rationale in class-level Javadoc.
ExplorationService interface documents why three exploration modes are bundled together and explicitly names the future split point, making the design decision auditable.
model/ package-info.java explicitly prohibits persistence, I/O, and HTTP concerns — boundary enforcement is documented and predictable.

GrammarConfigSerializer lives in api/ rather than config/, creating an asymmetry: the loader is in config/, the serializer is across the boundary in api/. Any non-HTTP consumer of GrammarConfig would need to reach into api/ for serialization utilities.

viz/ is a single-class package (RadialPlot only). A single-class package adds structure overhead without meaningful isolation gain; the class's domain role (SVG generation) is orthogonal to all other packages.

exploration/ (service logic) and model/exploration/ (DTOs) form a dual-layer that requires knowing to look in two locations for exploration-related code. The split is documented but imposes navigational friction.

QueryExecutor interface has one production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) but its Javadoc explicitly names it a testability seam; StubQueryExecutor in tests confirms this purpose — 5+ test classes inject it via the interface

ExplorationService interface has one production implementation (SemanticFieldExplorer) but WordSketchApiServer exposes a full constructor accepting ExplorationService, and ExplorationHandlersTest injects it directly

SemanticFieldExplorer owns the single-seed exploreByPattern algorithm (~200 lines of real logic) directly, while delegating exploreMultiSeed to MultiSeedExplorer and compareCollocateProfiles to CollocateProfileComparator in thin one-line forwarding methods

MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private classes each with exactly one caller (SemanticFieldExplorer); decomposing algorithms into separate files adds navigation overhead without cross-cutting reuse

SingleSeedExplorationOptions wraps ExplorationOptions as a field (composition), causing callers to access shared params as opts.base().topCollocates(), opts.base().minLogDice() rather than opts.topCollocates() — the wrapping layer adds one indirection level for a 4-parameter record

Static utility classes (RelationPatternBuilder, GrammarConfigSerializer, HttpApiUtils, ExploreResponseAssembler) all contain real logic and earn their place — no pass-through or re-export facades found

HttpApiUtils co-locates response-writing and request-parsing in one package-private class; the Javadoc justifies this and the class adds genuine error-handling, CORS, and validation policy beyond delegation
GrammarConfigSerializer separates JSON serialization from config data carriers (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig remain plain records) — legitimate isolating abstraction

CollocateQueryHelper is a package-private helper extracting grouped-collocate search logic shared across multiple BlackLabQueryExecutor methods — reduces duplication across executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern
pom.xml declares exactly 5 production-scope dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser, fastjson2, slf4j-api, jspecify — no bloat
logback-classic is runtime-scoped, correct for a logging implementation

All declared dependencies are demonstrably used: fastjson2 in GrammarConfigLoader/HttpApiUtils/CorpusQueryHandlers/VisualizationHandlers, jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable in 20 files, BlackLab throughout query/ layer
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose: single JSON library (fastjson2), single logging stack (slf4j + logback), no multiple HTTP clients
All versions are explicitly pinned via properties (blacklab.version=4.0.0, fastjson.version=2.0.57, junit.version=5.9.0) — no floating ranges
BlackLab 4.0.0 is resolved from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/ — a GitHub raw URL that is not a published Maven registry
The pom.xml itself documents this risk: 'This URL has no SLA and can become unavailable (rate-limited, branch deleted, or repo renamed)'

com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange and HttpServer are imported in 7 files: WordSketchApiServer, HttpApiUtils, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers
WordSketchApiServer declares 'private com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer server' and creates it via com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer.create() — the entire HTTP layer depends on this internal JDK API
com.sun.net.httpserver is NOT declared in pom.xml; it is an implicit dependency from the JDK's com.sun.* namespace, which is not a stable public contract

SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples uses Collectors.collectingAndThen(Collectors.toMap(...), m -> m.values().stream().limit(...)) to deduplicate by sentence — the intent is not immediately readable; a simple for-loop with a LinkedHashMap would state the purpose directly.

MultiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection builds four maps (seedCollocScores, seedCollocFreqs, collocLdSum, collocLdCount) in a single explicit for-loop, then uses collocLdSum.forEach((lemma, sum) -> avgLdMap.put(lemma, sum / collocLdCount.get(lemma))) as a separate inline lambda pass — the mixed for/forEach style adds a context switch for a reader tracing the aggregation logic.

CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits loop body performs sentence trimming, XML extraction, plain-text extraction, and collocate-lemma extraction all inline in a single for-loop — approximately 15 lines of transformations per iteration; a parseHit helper would make the loop declarative.
Phase structure (collectHits, scoreHits, buildAndRankCollocates) is cleanly separated; all three methods have focused bodies and clear naming.
BlackLabSnippetParser methods are each <15 lines with a single clearly-stated responsibility; guards are necessary and placed at entry, not scattered.
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern phases are explicitly labeled with section comments and helper method names; control flow is linear and easy to follow.

ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-presentation translation for ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult, CollocateProfile, and Edge in one class, keeping handlers free of serialisation logic.
ExplorationService interface insulates ExplorationHandlers from SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator; HTTP handlers depend only on the service contract.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling applies a uniform IAE→400, IOException→500 error propagation contract at every endpoint boundary without requiring per-handler try/catch.
buildCoreExploreResponse + parseCommonExploreParams extract the shared request-parsing and envelope-assembly preamble for the three explore handlers so the orchestration is readable.

ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest calls grammarConfig.relation(req.relation()) directly without routing through RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias, unlike sibling ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig which does so — short-form aliases such as adj_predicate silently fall back to a proximity pattern on /api/concordance/examples but resolve correctly on /api/semantic-field/examples.

handleSemanticFieldExamples assembles its response with 7 inline response.put() calls while sibling explore handlers all delegate to buildCoreExploreResponse + ExploreResponseAssembler.populate*Response — two patterns exist for the same boundary.

populateExploreResponse and populateComparisonResponse are void mutators of a map returned by buildCoreExploreResponse, so the handoff between the two steps is implicit: callers must know that populate* mutates the map from buildCore*.

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial logs the full raw request body at debug level (line 44: logger.debug("Radial: body = {}", body)) immediately followed by logging a single extracted field (line 53: logger.debug("Radial: center = {}", center)) — classic nosy entry/dump pattern.

RelationConfig.deriveDeprel() carries a 10-line 'Nullability design note' Javadoc embedded in a method-level comment for a 12-line implementation. The note justifies the @Nullable return type vs Optional — a design rationale that could be a single sentence rather than a multi-paragraph justification.

SemanticFieldExplorer (364 lines) uses banner-style section dividers (// ==================== EXPLORATION MODE ====================) three times. The class is not large enough to need this structuring; the well-named methods provide sufficient orientation.

HttpApiUtils class-level Javadoc includes a multi-sentence defensive justification for why two concerns are co-located ('Splitting them into two separate classes would not reduce coupling...'). Reads as over-justification of an unremarkable design choice.

GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader() Javadoc (lines 70-84) includes a multi-line embedded code example for a 4-line method that simply transfers a reader to a StringWriter before delegating — the example adds ceremony without commensurate insight.

GrammarConfigLoader.java:159: comment '// Canonical field name is "pattern"; cql_pattern is no longer supported' — the old field name is explicitly rejected with no fallback read path; parseRelation throws IllegalArgumentException when 'pattern' is absent, forcing any old configs to be updated.

GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys actively rejects the 'copulas' key with an explicit error message directing users to the new pattern-embedded approach — a completed migration guard with zero backward-compat shim.

SemanticFieldExplorer lives in 'exploration/' package, MultiSeedExplorer was extracted from it (Javadoc on MultiSeedExplorer says 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer') — the extraction is complete, both classes have clear ownership and no lingering references to their old arrangement.
No @Deprecated annotations in any production class; grep across all .java files returns zero hits.
No TODO/FIXME comments referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after' anywhere in src/main/java.
src/test/resources/baseline/metadata.txt contains 'Executor: legacy' but this is a fixture metadata label only — no test or production code references it as a class name or switch value.

RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES maps short aliases (adj_modifier, predicate, etc.) to canonical config IDs — this is a stable resolution layer, not a migration shim; it's the permanent public API surface for HTTP callers.

QueryExecutor interface's two default convenience methods (executeSurfacePatternForRelation, executeBcqlQueryForRelation) are helpers that reduce boilerplate at call sites, not compatibility shims — they delegate directly to the current API with no translation for a removed or deprecated path.
Top-level packages (api/, config/, exploration/, indexer/blacklab/, model/, model/exploration/, query/, utils/, viz/) each have a single-purpose name that accurately describes contents.
api/ has 11 files (10 production): all HTTP handlers, utilities, assemblers, and the server wiring — coherent, not mixed-concern.
model/exploration/ has 11 files (10 production): all exploration-specific DTOs (CollocateProfile, ComparisonResult, ExplorationOptions, etc.) — cohesive.

FetchExamplesOptions lives in model/ (flat) but its own Javadoc states it 'follows the options-object pattern used by sibling types (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions)' — both siblings reside in model/exploration/. All three are used exclusively by the exploration service layer (ExplorationService, SemanticFieldExplorer, ExplorationHandlers).
viz/ contains exactly one production file (RadialPlot.java) used only by api/VisualizationHandlers.java. A single-file package is a thin structural unit at this project scale.
No coupling-direction inversions observed: api/ -> model/, api/ -> config/, api/ -> exploration/, exploration/ -> query/, exploration/ -> config/ are all correct downstream-to-upstream flows.
GrammarConfigSerializer is intentionally placed in api/ to keep the JSON library dependency out of config/ — the Javadoc documents this rationale explicitly.
Main.java at the word_sketch root is a CLI entry point with high fan-out across all packages — appropriate placement.
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: FetchExamplesOptions is the one concrete placement outlier: it is an exploration options record (documented as a sibling to ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions) but lives one level up in model/ instead of model/exploration/. A developer browsing model/exploration/ looking for the full set of exploration option types will not find it there. The viz/ single-file package is minor but adds a thin, isolated structural unit.

evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType javadoc says 'Logs a warning when the field is present but unrecognised' but the implementation throws IllegalArgumentException — opposite behavior from what is documented

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples sets bcqlQuery = rel.get().pattern() (unresolved template) for the non-fallback path, but the executed query is the resolved form (via executeBcqlQueryForRelation); response['bcql'] therefore shows the unexecuted template rather than the actual query

QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern javadoc says 'The headword lemma must be embedded in bcqlPattern' — implying a hard requirement — but the BlackLabQueryExecutor implementation silently returns an empty list when lemma extraction fails, with no exception thrown. Sibling method executeCollocations documents its null-input behavior explicitly; executeSurfacePattern does not

QueryExecutor interface and its implementations are otherwise well-aligned: @Nullable lemma on executeCollocations matches the documented 'returns empty list silently', error throws match declarations, return types match all paths
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling, requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam all throw IllegalArgumentException as documented and consistently honored across all handler callers
ExplorationService interface method declarations and SemanticFieldExplorer implementations match: @NonNull annotations, IOException throws, and delegation behavior all consistent

BlackLabConllUIndexer.close(): `if (indexer != null)` guards a final field — indexer is always non-null when close() is reachable on a successfully constructed object (constructor throws if assignment fails); the check is unreachable-false

CollocateQueryHelper.extractCollocateLemma: `if (collocatePos > 0)` duplicates the `position < 1` guard already present in extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition — both layers are intentional but the outer guard is the one that matters for call-site clarity
Control flow throughout BlackLabQueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, and MultiSeedExplorer is direct and phase-structured with clear guard clauses before work
Condition logic in buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution, executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern is straightforward: early returns on invalid input, no dead code after unconditional returns
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: null/empty guard on `seed` comes after reading from `opts` fields, but opts is @NonNull so no issue; guard position is readable
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: One redundant null check on a final field in BlackLabConllUIndexer.close() creates a misleading impression that indexer could be null at close-time. While harmless at runtime, it signals uncertainty about the constructor contract where none exists.

evidence: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable used consistently on public method parameters and return types across api/, query/, exploration/, and config/ packages

CollocateQueryHelper.java: field 'private final BlackLabIndex index' carries no @Nullable annotation, yet the no-arg constructor assigns this.index = null. The @NonNull annotation appears only on the primary constructor parameter — it does not transfer to the field. A developer reading only the field declaration cannot determine whether null is a valid state without reading both constructors.

RelationConfig.java: record component 'Optional<RelationType> relationType' stores Optional in a field, which violates the Java API Design Note that Optional is designed for use as a return type, not for stored fields or record components. @Nullable RelationType relationType with an Optional-returning accessor would be more idiomatic and avoids breaking serialization conventions.

RelationPatternBuilder.buildFullPattern(RelationConfig, String headword): Javadoc states 'if null/blank, the original pattern is returned unchanged', so null IS valid, but the parameter carries no @Nullable annotation. Callers relying on annotations alone would not discover this contract without reading the implementation.
QueryResults.CollocateResult correctly annotates @Nullable String collocateLemma, matching actual runtime behavior where null signals no extractable lemma.
Sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits clause (SnippetResult, CollocateResult) gives strong compile-time exhaustiveness guarantees.
GrammarConfig.relation(String id) returns Optional<RelationConfig> consistently for all query-style lookups — correct use of Optional as a return type.
Domain vocabulary is coherent and consistent: 'seed', 'collocate', 'logDice', 'relation', 'exploration', 'deprel' are used uniformly across all layers (handlers, service, model, query).
Method names clearly express phase and intent: fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates, buildAndRankCollocates, collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups.
ComparisonResult has a public accessor collocates() but its backing private field is named 'adjectives' — POS-specific name leaking into a type designed for any collocate kind.

CollocateProfile record declares its primary identifying field as 'adjective' (line: 'String adjective,') while the class Javadoc says 'Profile of one collocate' — field name encodes a POS assumption in a general type.

RelationEdgeType enum has constants SEED_ADJ and DISCOVERED_ADJ whose 'ADJ' suffix implies adjective-only edges, but these types are used by ExploreResponseAssembler for any exploration relation (noun or verb collocates too).

MultiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection uses collocLdSum, collocLdCount, avgLdMap and local variable avgLd — 'ld' abbreviation for logDice is used nowhere else in the codebase; every other file writes full 'logDice' (minLogDice, avgLogDice, collocateLogDice).

ExplorationHandlers uses 'relationConfig' in handleSemanticFieldExplore but 'resolvedConfig' in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti and handleSemanticFieldExamples for the same variable returned from the same resolveRelationConfig() helper.
Handler method prefix 'handle' is used uniformly across all handler classes — an intentional and well-applied convention.
Inner request records (ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, CommonExploreParams) are precisely named and scoped.
Class names ExploreResponseAssembler, CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, SemanticFieldExplorer are descriptive and role-appropriate.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: The adjective-centric naming leak (CollocateProfile.adjective field, ComparisonResult.adjectives backing field, RelationEdgeType.SEED_ADJ/DISCOVERED_ADJ enum names) is systemic — it originates in the comparison subsystem's initial adjective-only design and has not been fully updated as the abstraction was generalized. While each instance is individually small, together they form a vocabulary schism where 'adjective' and 'collocate' refer to the same concept depending on which layer you are reading. The logDice abbreviation inconsistency in MultiSeedExplorer and the variable-name mismatch in ExplorationHandlers add smaller additional drag.

evidence: HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling maps IllegalArgumentException→400, RequestEntityTooLargeException→413, IOException→500, Exception→500 — a single coherent gateway used by every registered route

All handler classes (ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, SketchHandlers) throw only IllegalArgumentException for validation failures and IOException for corpus errors; the boundary contract is applied uniformly
All catch-and-rethrow in BlackLabQueryExecutor and CollocateQueryHelper preserve the original cause: 'throw new IOException("BCQL parse error: " + e.getMessage(), e)' is the consistent pattern

BlackLabConllUIndexer constructor and close() both use 'catch (Exception e)' — broad enough to swallow NullPointerException, ClassCastException, or other programming errors and re-raise them as IOException('Failed to create/finalize index'), masking the real failure type
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches IOException per relation, logs a warning, and accumulates errors in a response-level list — intentional partial-result semantics, not silent swallowing

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() wraps IOException in IllegalStateException — documented two-tier contract; Javadoc explicitly explains when to use load() vs createDefaultEnglish() and callers in Main use load() with distinct catch blocks for FileNotFoundException vs IOException
RequestEntityTooLargeException extends RuntimeException and is consistently mapped to 413 by wrapWithErrorHandling — a clean custom exception type for one specific HTTP boundary condition

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType throws IllegalArgumentException with the full set of valid values in the message ('valid values: ...') when an unrecognised string appears — context is preserved and actionable
Dependency hierarchy is acyclic and flows cleanly: utils/model → config → query/exploration → api; no package depends upward on a higher layer
model package is imported by 6+ packages but has zero outward cross-module dependencies — an appropriate hub role for a DTO layer

QueryExecutor interface (query package) imports config.RelationConfig and config.RelationPatternBuilder for two default convenience methods (executeSurfacePatternForRelation, executeBcqlQueryForRelation), while BlackLabQueryExecutor (the sole concrete implementation) imports neither config class — the interface is coupled to a layer its implementation is not

WordSketchApiServer imports both exploration.ExplorationService (interface) and exploration.SemanticFieldExplorer (concrete class) — the concrete import exists solely for a convenience constructor that instantiates SemanticFieldExplorer internally, anchoring the api layer to the concrete implementation

ExplorationService interface cleanly abstracts the exploration subsystem boundary — all api-layer handler classes depend only on ExplorationService, not on SemanticFieldExplorer, except WordSketchApiServer itself
No import cycles detected across any package pairing; indexer.blacklab and viz are isolated with no inbound production dependencies from other packages
WordSketchApiServer defers port binding to start() and explicitly documents this separation in Javadoc: 'Port binding is deferred to start() so that construction and network binding are separated.'
GrammarConfig is loaded explicitly in Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand and injected through constructors; no global singleton holds it.
cors.allow.origin is read via System.getProperty on every call inside setCorsHeader(), not cached at class load — the Javadoc explains this is intentional so test overrides take effect immediately.

BlackLabConllUIndexer constructor calls BlackLab.openForWriting(..., formatName), which silently requires DocumentFormats.addConfigFormatsInDirectories to have been called first. That registration is the caller's responsibility (Main.handleBlackLabIndexCommand calls it immediately before constructing the indexer), but BlackLabConllUIndexer does not enforce or document this precondition in its own constructor.

Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand reads System.getProperty('grammar.config', 'grammars/relations.json') inline, then calls GrammarConfigLoader.load(). GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() is a separate factory that performs the identical property read with the identical default. The two paths are independent: createDefaultEnglish() is only called from tests (RelationsValidationTest, HybridAlgorithmTest), while Main uses load() directly. If the default path or property name ever changes, both sites must be updated.

GrammarConfigHelper (test helper) uses a non-volatile, unsynchronized static field for lazy init: 'private static GrammarConfig testConfig; if (testConfig == null) { testConfig = ...; }'. Two threads calling getTestConfig() concurrently could both observe null and both load, with the winner's reference potentially not visible to the loser under the JMM without volatile.
All Java naming conventions are applied uniformly: camelCase methods/fields, PascalCase classes, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE constants — no drift anywhere in the codebase.
Handler classes in api/ are all package-private (ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) — consistent encapsulation pattern.
Mixed record-vs-class strategy in model/ is documented in model/package-info.java with explicit rationale, making it intentional rather than drift.

GrammarConfigSerializer is public while ExploreResponseAssembler is package-private; both are api-internal assembler utilities. Difference is justified: GrammarConfigSerializer is imported from config test package (GrammarConfigLoaderTest), so public visibility is required.
FetchExamplesOptions lives in model/ but its docstring calls ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions its 'sibling types', which are in model.exploration/ — misplaced by one package level.

viz/ package is the only package without a package-info.java file; all seven other packages (api, config, exploration, indexer.blacklab, model, model.exploration, query, utils) have package-info.java.
holistic_context.conventions.naming_by_directory and sibling_behavior are both empty — mechanical scanner found no cross-directory naming drift.
~35 test classes cover ~44 production classes; nearly every non-trivial module has a corresponding test file

StubQueryExecutor is a well-designed shared test double (all methods return empty by default, overridden via anonymous subclasses) that avoids mock frameworks and keeps tests decoupled from implementation details
TestExchangeFactory.MockExchange provides a reusable HttpExchange stub, enabling handler tests that don't start a real HTTP server (except WordSketchApiServerTest, which does start one)
Live-index tests (@Disabled + Assumptions.assumeTrue) are correctly gated — they won't silently pass in CI, but can be activated via CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var
Handler tests cover both happy path and all major error paths (400 missing param, 400 bad relation, 413 body too large, 500 IOException)
No snapshot tests; all assertions are value-based

BcqlPatternUtils.findLabelTokenIndex has non-trivial label-finding logic used in BlackLabQueryExecutor and CollocateQueryHelper, but has no direct unit test — only indirect coverage via higher-level tests
BlackLabQueryExecutor core execution paths (executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeBcqlQuery) have zero runnable CI tests — all live-index tests are @Disabled
CqlParserIntegrationTest does exercise BlackLab's CorpusQueryLanguageParser directly (not @Disabled), providing thin but real coverage of the CQL parsing layer
WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real HTTP server and exercises route registration and lifecycle, but uses StubQueryExecutor so query compilation is not exercised end-to-end
MathUtils.round2dp and BcqlPatternUtils.extractHeadword have no dedicated tests (extractHeadword used inside SemanticFieldExplorerTest stubs but the method itself is not verified)
All exploration endpoints share identical parameter names: seed/seeds, relation, top, min_logdice, min_shared — no drift across ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, or CorpusQueryHandlers
Error contracts are explicit and uniform: IllegalArgumentException → 400, IOException → 500, RequestEntityTooLargeException → 413, enforced by wrapWithErrorHandling across every handler
explore, explore-multi, and compare all use buildCoreExploreResponse which nests operational params in a parameters sub-object; /api/semantic-field/examples breaks this pattern with a flat envelope
ExplorationService.fetchExamples declares its second parameter as 'headword' while the HTTP handler (ExplorationHandlers) and all sibling methods use 'seed' for the same concept

ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest extracts seed then collocate; ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples extracts collocate then seed — and the response field order follows the extraction order, inverting seed/collocate position in two sibling endpoints
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: loopback-only bind, all endpoints unauthenticated, with a clear warning that changing the bind address requires adding auth
start() calls InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — the loopback-only constraint is enforced in code, not just docs
HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() is called from every response-sending method (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse) — CORS origin is applied uniformly with no gaps
CORS wildcard (*) detection: setCorsHeader() logs a warn when cors.allow.origin is *, showing awareness that this widens the browser trust boundary across all endpoints
registerHandler() enforces HTTP method (GET vs POST) uniformly for every registered route via shared logic; no endpoint bypasses this
wrapWithErrorHandling() is applied to every handler registration in registerRoutes() — no unguarded handler

Input validation is applied consistently via requireParam (throws IAE → 400) across all parameter-accepting endpoints: explore uses it for seed, explore-multi for seeds, concordance for seed+collocate, bcql validates pattern length and bracket depth

CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest validates both pattern length (MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH=1024) and bracket complexity (MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH=20) — the highest-risk endpoint (arbitrary query execution) receives the strictest input controls
No mixed trust boundaries: there are no endpoints with auth decorators/middleware alongside unauthenticated siblings — the model is uniformly 'trust the local caller'

WordSketchApiServer delegates to 5 focused handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) — each handler owns one domain; the server owns only routing and lifecycle.

RelationConfig is a pure data record; RelationPatternBuilder holds all pattern-building logic; GrammarConfig holds data while GrammarConfigLoader handles I/O — data/logic separation applied consistently across the config layer.
SemanticFieldExplorer acts as a stable facade that delegates to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator, insulating the HTTP layer from algorithm internals.
CollocateQueryHelper was extracted cleanly from BlackLabQueryExecutor, separating low-level BlackLab query mechanics (grouping, scoring) from the executor's dispatch logic.
ExplorationHandlers.buildCoreExploreResponse and parseCommonExploreParams abstract the shared parameter-parsing and response-building structure used across three handler methods.

POS regex literals are duplicated across three classes: SemanticFieldExplorer (FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT = '[xpos="NN.*"]'), CollocateProfileComparator (FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = '[xpos="JJ.*"]'), and RelationPatternBuilder.buildCollocateReversePattern (a switch returning the same literals). RelationPatternBuilder already owns the canonical PosGroup→regex mapping; the other two classes bypass it.

buildCoreExploreResponse(String relationType, int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, Map<String, Object> extraParams, Map<String, Object> variantFields) uses two positional Map<String, Object> parameters that differ only in where they land in the response envelope (nested vs. top-level). No compile-time constraint prevents callers from swapping them.

strengths: Domain-aligned package structure (api/, config/, model/, exploration/, query/, indexer/) lets a new engineer locate code by feature without prior knowledge of the system.

API layer decomposes into one focused handler class per concern area (sketch, exploration, concordance, corpus query, visualization), composed by a thin routing server — change boundaries are predictable.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader data-vs-loader split is clean and documented with explicit rationale, preventing config-data classes from accumulating I/O or library dependencies.
ExplorationService interface exposes a coherent public surface for all exploration modes with documented reasoning for why they are bundled and where a future split would occur.
Package-level contracts are written down (package-info.java files) and enforced in practice — e.g., model/ bans HTTP/IO concerns, and the code holds to that.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are placement asymmetries rather than structural failures: one serializer class that belongs one package to the left, one thin single-class package that adds structure overhead, and a dual-layer model/service split that adds navigational friction without obscuring ownership.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly above the '60=significant drag' threshold and approaches the '80=solid but uneven' anchor. The domain decomposition is genuinely clear and the documented rationales are a structural asset that most codebases lack. What prevents a higher score is a small cluster of placement asymmetries (GrammarConfigSerializer in api/ instead of config/, single-class viz/ package, dual exploration/ vs model/exploration/ navigation) that a developer would notice as minor surprises when first mapping the structure. These don't obscure ownership or make change prediction unreliable — they are rough edges rather than architectural problems — which places the score at the upper end of the '80=solid but uneven' band, specifically 79.

strengths: Both single-implementation interfaces (QueryExecutor, ExplorationService) are explicitly documented as testability seams with confirmed test-time injection, placing them firmly in the skip category of the rubric

Static utility classes (RelationPatternBuilder, GrammarConfigSerializer, HttpApiUtils, ExploreResponseAssembler) each add real logic — policy enforcement, pattern construction, serialization — rather than merely forwarding calls
CollocateQueryHelper genuinely reduces duplication by extracting grouped-collocate search logic shared across three methods of BlackLabQueryExecutor
The model package is cleanly decomposed into pure DTOs (records) and objects with derived accessors, with no HTTP or persistence concerns leaking in
QueryExecutor default convenience methods (executeSurfacePatternForRelation, executeBcqlQueryForRelation) add genuine value by keeping pattern-construction logic out of HTTP handlers
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The main abstraction drag comes from an asymmetric decomposition pattern in the exploration layer: SemanticFieldExplorer owns the single-seed algorithm directly (~200 lines) while delegating multi-seed and comparison as thin one-liners to package-private helpers that have no other callers, creating navigation overhead without cross-cutting reuse.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase avoids the most costly abstraction anti-patterns: no pass-through wrappers, no util dumping grounds, no facades that define no logic, and the single-implementation interfaces are justified by confirmed test usage. The drag comes from an asymmetric design in the exploration subsystem where SemanticFieldExplorer acts as both algorithm owner and coordination facade simultaneously, and from a minor options-wrapping layer (SingleSeedExplorationOptions wrapping ExplorationOptions) that adds one level of indirection for a 4-parameter API. These issues would occasionally slow a developer navigating the exploration package but do not make the overall abstraction posture unreliable. This puts the codebase solidly in the 80-85 range: mostly strong with a few layers that feel slightly overbuilt.

strengths: Declared dependency set is lean: 5 production runtime dependencies covering exactly the needed domains (corpus engine, JSON, logging, nullability annotations) with no overlap
All explicitly declared production dependencies are actively used throughout the codebase — no dead weight in pom.xml
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose: one JSON library (fastjson2), one logging framework (slf4j + logback), consistent across all modules
All dependency versions are pinned via properties (blacklab.version, fastjson.version, etc.) — no floating version ranges that could introduce non-reproducible builds
Test dependencies (JUnit 5) are correctly scoped to 'test' and logback is runtime-scoped — scope hygiene is correct throughout
dependency_health.issue_character: The issues are two distinct risks rather than pervasive decay: an undeclared structural dependency on a JDK-internal HTTP API embedded across the entire API layer, and a single external dependency hosted on an unreliable GitHub raw URL with no SLA.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The declared dependency set is genuinely clean — lean, purposeful, non-overlapping, and correctly scoped — which pushes well above 60. However, the entire HTTP API layer (7 files) is built on com.sun.net.httpserver, an internal JDK API that is not a published contract, is not declared in pom.xml, and would need a multi-file refactor to replace. Additionally, one of the two core domain dependencies (BlackLab 4.0.0) is resolved from a GitHub raw URL with no SLA, a risk the pom.xml itself documents but does not mitigate. These two issues — one systemic (invisible JDK-internal HTTP dependency) and one operational (fragile resolver URL) — prevent the otherwise clean declared-dependency profile from reaching 80.

strengths: BlackLabSnippetParser methods are consistently short, single-purpose, and have entry guards placed at the top — a developer can read any one of them without context from the others.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern uses phase-labeled helper extraction (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) that reads as a clear narrative of the algorithm.
State mutation is consistently local and bounded: maps are initialized at the top of each method, populated in a controlled loop, and returned — no shared mutable fields between methods.

GrammarConfigLoader uses small, name-communicating private methods (parseAndValidateVersion, validateNoLegacyKeys, parseRelations, parseRelation) rather than one large parse method, keeping each branch at a readable depth.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated to two or three specific local patterns: an opaque stream-collector chain used once for deduplication, a mixed for/forEach aggregation in one method, and one loop body that does too many inline text transformations — none are systemic, but each requires a brief decode pause.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase is mostly well-crafted at the function level: methods are proportionate, extraction is consistent, and state mutation is bounded throughout. The few rough edges — particularly the opaque Collectors.collectingAndThen deduplication in fetchExamples and the mixed aggregation style in findCollocateIntersection — are real friction points where a developer must pause to decode intent rather than read it directly. These are isolated occurrences rather than recurring patterns, which places quality above the 80 mark (solid but uneven) but below 85 (strong with minor rough edges), landing at 81.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler is a properly-placed single-responsibility translation layer: all model→JSON conversions for exploration results are in one class, preventing translation entropy from leaking into handlers.
ExplorationService interface is a clean testability seam that hides the SemanticFieldExplorer/MultiSeedExplorer/CollocateProfileComparator composition from the HTTP layer.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling enforces a uniform error-propagation contract across all endpoint boundaries without tangled per-handler try/catch.
buildCoreExploreResponse and parseCommonExploreParams correctly extract the shared request-parsing preamble, making each explore handler's orchestration read as composition of collaborators.
GrammarConfigLoader produces immutable GrammarConfig value objects so callers depend on loaded data, not on file-IO machinery — the config boundary is correctly separated.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are localized to the API layer's internal seam design: one sibling handler (ConcordanceHandlers) diverges from the established pattern in a concrete functional way (missing alias resolution), and one handler (handleSemanticFieldExamples) deviates from the shared response-assembly protocol used by all other explore handlers.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates deliberately architected seams — a clean service interface, a centralised translation layer, and uniform error propagation — that a developer would find mostly reliable and unsurprising. What pulls the score below 80 is a concrete functional inconsistency at the ConcordanceHandlers boundary: alias resolution is missing, causing different behavior for the same request parameter on two overlapping endpoints, which is the kind of implicit contract difference that a developer discovers only through runtime testing. The inline response assembly in handleSemanticFieldExamples is a secondary pattern inconsistency that would create confusion when extending the API. These are module-scoped issues, not pervasive, placing this in the solid-but-uneven 70s range rather than the recurring-friction 60s.

strengths: Comments throughout explain WHY, not just WHAT — e.g., the two-tier exception contract rationale in GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish(), the CORS wildcard warning rationale in HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader(), the testing-seam explanation on the package-private SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern() overload.

No trivial pass-through wrappers — methods like handleSurfaceRelations/handleDepRelations that delegate to handleRelationsForType pass a semantic enum value that meaningfully configures behaviour; they are not empty forwarders.

ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The issues are isolated to a handful of locations — one handler with two consecutive debug statements that dump then re-extract the same request, and docstring verbosity in two or three methods that reads as defensive over-justification rather than genuine documentation; there is no systemic AI-hallmark noise spreading across the codebase.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly in the 80–90 range on this dimension: a developer reading through these files would find the code purpose-driven and free of ceremony almost everywhere, with a small number of spots where docstring length or the debug-log pair in VisualizationHandlers produces minor friction. The issues are local and isolated rather than recurring across the codebase, which distinguishes this from the 'developer regularly encounters noise' tier at 80. The minor pockets of over-explanation and the nosy debug pair just prevent a 90+ rating.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface has thorough, accurate JavaDoc for each method; null-input behavior for executeCollocations is explicitly and correctly documented

contract_coherence.issue_character: Issues are isolated doc/behavior mismatches rather than a systemic pattern: one method's docstring describes a warning where the code throws, one HTTP response field echoes a template rather than the resolved query, and one method's contract says 'must be embedded' but the failure mode is a silent empty return.

strengths: Phase-structured algorithms (CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits / scoreHits, SemanticFieldExplorer phase helpers) make control flow easy to follow — each phase does exactly what its name says
Guard clauses are applied consistently before doing work: null/empty lemma checks in executeCollocations and executeDependencyPattern return early with log lines rather than causing downstream errors

logic_clarity.issue_character: The single defect is an isolated, harmless redundant null check on a final field; it does not indicate systemic confusion about logic conditions and has no functional consequence.

strengths: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable applied consistently on public API boundaries across all major packages — a developer can trust annotations on method signatures as accurate
Sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits correctly models sum types, giving compile-time exhaustiveness guarantees on pattern matching

Phase-oriented method naming in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildNounToCollocatesMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) makes the algorithm readable as prose.

naming_quality.issue_character: The naming issues are not random but form a single coherent pattern: the comparison/exploration subsystem was originally designed for adjective collocates only, and that origin is still visible in field names (CollocateProfile.adjective, ComparisonResult.adjectives), enum constants (SEED_ADJ, DISCOVERED_ADJ), and log strings — while the public API surface was later generalized to 'collocate'. A score of 77 reflects a codebase with clearly good naming discipline that carries one cohesive subsystem-level residue and two minor local inconsistencies.

strengths: Uniform two-tier exception contract across the entire codebase: IllegalArgumentException for validation failures, IOException for I/O and parse failures — applied consistently from QueryExecutor interface through all handler classes

Single error gateway (HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling) handles all HTTP error mapping; every route registration passes through it, so the HTTP status codes are predictable from the exception type alone

error_consistency.score_rationale: The overall error strategy is coherent and well-enforced — a developer reading one handler can correctly predict the error behavior of any other handler, which pushes toward the 90 band. strengths: The package hierarchy is acyclic and layered: utils/model at the bottom, config above them, query and exploration in the middle, api at the top — a developer can trace dependency direction without surprises

indexer.blacklab and viz are isolated subsystems with no incoming dependencies from query or api, correctly scoping their concerns

cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The overall dependency direction is coherent and consistent across the codebase, with no cycles and clearly defined layers — a developer navigating this codebase can trust that dependency arrows point downward. convention_outlier.issue_character: The two deviations found are isolated placement/annotation omissions rather than systemic naming or structural drift: one options class lives one package level too high, and one small package lacks its annotation file.

Neither issue meaningfully impedes a developer, keeping the score well above 80 but short of the 85+ range where all observable deviations would need to be absent or trivially negligible.

issues.summary: GrammarConfigSerializer lives in api/ but serializes config-layer objects, creating a cross-layer placement asymmetry
issues.suggestion: Move GrammarConfigSerializer to a config/serialization/ subpackage or config/ directly, accepting the fastjson2 dependency in config/. Alternatively, introduce a dedicated api/serializers/ sub-package to cleanly separate HTTP serializers from handler routing. Either resolves the asymmetry without touching the data/loader split.
evidence: GrammarConfigSerializer converts GrammarConfig/RelationConfig (config-layer types) to JSONObject. Its Javadoc explains the placement: 'Serialization lives here rather than on the value objects so that GrammarConfig and RelationConfig remain pure data carriers with no dependency on the JSON library.'

However, the result is that the loader is in config/ while the serializer is in api/. Any non-HTTP consumer of GrammarConfig needing JSON output would be forced to import from api/, violating expected dependency direction.
The api/ package-info says the package contains 'HTTP request handlers, routing utilities, and response assembly' — a standalone serializer utility is a broadening of that responsibility.

issues.summary: viz/ package contains exactly one class (RadialPlot) with no sibling types, adding structural overhead with no isolation benefit
issues.suggestion: Merge RadialPlot into api/ as a package-private helper used by VisualizationHandlers, or if SVG generation is expected to grow, rename the package to api/visualization/ and move VisualizationHandlers there too. This eliminates the thin standalone package without losing the isolation intent.
evidence: viz/ contains only RadialPlot.java — a pure SVG generation utility. There are no interfaces, helpers, or related types alongside it.
RadialPlot is a 400+ line class with inline spiral-layout constants — its domain (SVG rendering) is orthogonal to every other package in the system.
VisualizationHandlers in api/ presumably delegates to RadialPlot, meaning the usage is exclusively HTTP-response-building, which would make it a natural companion to api/ or a sub-package thereof.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer mixes direct algorithm ownership with delegation, making its responsibility boundary ambiguous
issues.suggestion: Choose a consistent boundary: either (a) inline MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator back into SemanticFieldExplorer as private methods (they have no other callers and are already package-private), or (b) extract the single-seed algorithm into its own package-private SingleSeedExplorer class so all three algorithms live in parallel sibling classes and SemanticFieldExplorer becomes a pure coordination facade. Option (a) is simpler given the current scale.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern contains ~200 lines of single-seed algorithm logic directly in the class

issues.summary: RelationConfig stores Optional<RelationType> as a record component instead of using @Nullable

exploreByPattern, exploreMultiSeed, and compareCollocateProfiles all use 'seed'/'seeds' as their primary noun parameter

issues.summary: POS regex literals duplicated in SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator instead of using RelationPatternBuilder's canonical mapping
issues.suggestion: Add a package-accessible static method to RelationPatternBuilder (e.g., buildFallbackPattern(PosGroup)) that returns the regex string for a given POS group, reusing the existing switch. Replace FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT in SemanticFieldExplorer with RelationPatternBuilder.buildFallbackPattern(PosGroup.NOUN) and FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN in CollocateProfileComparator with RelationPatternBuilder.buildFallbackPattern(PosGroup.ADJ). This ensures all POS regex strings are maintained in one place.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer line 93: private static final String FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]";
CollocateProfileComparator line 30: private static final String FALLBACK_ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]";

RelationPatternBuilder.buildCollocateReversePattern already owns the canonical PosGroup→regex mapping (switch returning the same literal strings: ADJ→[xpos="JJ.*"], NOUN→[xpos="NN.*"]). The fallback values in the other two classes are structurally identical to entries in this switch and are never derived from it.

issues.summary: buildCoreExploreResponse takes two positional Map<String, Object> parameters with no type distinction between extra-params (nested) and variant-fields (top-level)
issues.suggestion: Extract a private record or builder: e.g., record ExploreResponseSpec(Map<String,Object> nestedParams, Map<String,Object> topLevelFields) {}, and require callers to construct it explicitly. This makes the two maps named rather than positional, eliminating the swap risk and documenting the intent at call sites. Alternatively, collapse the 6 parameters into a structured request record that callers populate via named fields.
evidence: Method signature: buildCoreExploreResponse(String relationType, int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, Map<String, Object> extraParams, Map<String, Object> variantFields)

extraParams is merged into the nested 'parameters' sub-object; variantFields is merged into the top-level response map. The two maps are structurally identical types but have different semantics enforced only by position.
Three call sites pass Map.of() for one or both parameters, which makes the distinction implicit and easy to misread when skimming call sites.
